q: What is causal inference trying to do?
a:
Use data to work out whether one thing actually causes another — typically in
settings where you'd like to run an experiment but can't.
title: Designing Research

q: Every car insurance company claims switchers save ~$500. Why can that be true for all of them?
a:
Because each only surveys its own switchers, and people switch only when that
company quotes them less than they currently pay. The sample is selected on having
saved.
title: Designing Research

q: Name the two distinct ways an analysis can give you a wrong answer.
a:
The data is bad (badly sourced, unreliable, weakly measured, or about the wrong
thing), or the data is fine but the design answers a different question than the one
you asked.
title: Designing Research

q: Which of those two failures is more dangerous, and why?
a:
The second. Bad data can be spotted by inspecting it. A design mismatch leaves every
step looking correct, so there's nothing to notice.
title: Designing Research

q: You have the insurance company's switcher data and want to know which company is cheapest. Would ten times more data help?
a:
No. More data makes the estimate of *switcher savings* more precise. It doesn't
change which quantity you're estimating. Design problems don't shrink with sample size.
title: Designing Research

q: What job does a research design do?
a:
It connects the question to the analysis — it's the reasoning that makes the number
you compute the number your question actually asked for.
title: Designing Research

q: What does it mean that data is good at telling you "what" but not "why"?
a:
Data reports what was observed. Turning that into a claim about how the world works
takes a design that rules out the other explanations for what you observed.
title: Designing Research

q: What's the difference between a theory and a research question?
a:
A theory is a claim about *why* the world works some way. A research question names
an observable *what* that, if you measured it, would inform the theory.
title: Research Questions

q: Why can't data answer a "why" question directly?
a:
Data reports what was observed. Multiple different whys can produce the same what,
so the observation alone doesn't pick between them.
title: Research Questions

q: Theory: cultural exposure makes people smarter. Why does finding a positive correlation fail to support it?
a:
The correlation is equally consistent with the reverse — more intelligent people
choosing more cultural exposure. The observation can't distinguish the two.
title: Research Questions

q: What's the pre-analysis test for whether a research question is any good?
a:
Imagine each result you could get, including contradictory ones. If a contradictory
result would make you make excuses rather than change your mind, the question isn't
testing your theory — and the confirming result wouldn't be evidence either.
title: Research Questions

q: You want to know the effect of the minimum wage and plan to randomly assign it across states. Which filter kills this?
a:
Feasibility. Nobody will let you do it. The theory survives; you need a different
question that reaches the same why.
title: Research Questions

q: Name the three practical filters on a research question.
a:
Feasibility (does the data exist, would anyone permit it), scale (money, time,
people), and design (does a real-world setting exist where your approach applies).
title: Research Questions

q: What's wrong with a paper on "the determinants of school dropout" listing 30 determinants?
a:
None of them are answered well, so the results are a mush you can't act on. One
narrow question answered properly is worth more.
title: Research Questions

q: How should you handle a question that's too big?
a:
Carve off a small, well-defined chunk that still bears on the theory — "does a free
pre-school lunch help you stay in school?" rather than "what causes dropout?"
title: Research Questions

q: What is a variable?
a:
One measurement taken repeatedly across many observations — the same thing recorded
each time, like the height of each flower in a field.
title: Types of Variables

q: What does it mean to "describe" a variable?
a:
Summarise what's in it — what's typical, how much it varies — so someone understands
it without reading every observation.
title: Types of Variables

q: Name the five types of variable.
a: Continuous, count, ordinal, categorical (including binary), and qualitative.
title: Types of Variables

q: What makes a variable continuous rather than a count?
a:
Continuous can take any value in a range with no "next" value — measure more
precisely and you get more decimals. A count is whole numbers only and can't be
negative.
title: Types of Variables

q: Education level and college major are both about education. Why are they different types?
a:
Education level is ordinal — the categories have an order. College major is
categorical — engineering isn't more than English, just different.
title: Types of Variables

q: Why can't you take the average of an ordinal variable?
a:
The categories have order but no meaningful spacing, so there's no scale to average
on. "The average of elementary, high school, and college" doesn't refer to anything.
title: Types of Variables

q: How does binary relate to categorical?
a: Binary is a categorical variable with exactly two options — left-handed or not.
title: Types of Variables

q: You want to analyse newspaper articles quantitatively. What do you have to do first?
a:
Code them into one of the other types — word count (count), topic tags (categorical)
— and accept you're now analysing the coding, not the text itself.
title: Types of Variables

q: Why does variable type matter at all?
a:
Because the description you use depends on the type. Choosing the wrong one gives
you numbers that are computable but meaningless.
title: Types of Variables

q: What is a distribution?
a:
A description of how often each possible value occurs. For a fair coin: 50% heads,
50% tails — that's the complete distribution.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: Why is a categorical variable so easy to summarize?
a:
Listing the proportion in each category *is* the full distribution. There's nothing
further to compress and nothing lost.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: Why can't you use the same approach for a continuous variable?
a:
Almost no two observations share an exact value, so the table would have one row per
observation — you'd just be looking at the dataset again.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: What's the relationship between a histogram and a density?
a:
A histogram bins a continuous variable and counts each bin. Shrink the bins toward
zero width and you get a density — the same idea, describing the likelihood of landing
near a point rather than a count within a bin.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: State the difference between what a mean tells you and what a median tells you.
a:
The mean is representative of the typical *value* you'd get back from the
distribution. The median is representative of the typical *observation* you'd
encounter.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: A hundred people, median income $30,000. Jeff Bezos walks in. What happens to the median, and to the mean?
a:
The median barely moves — to whoever was standing next to the middle person, maybe
$31,000. The mean rockets upward, because his income gets averaged in with everyone
else's.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: How is a standard deviation constructed?
a:
Subtract the mean from each value, square the differences, take the mean of those
squares (the variance), then square-root back into the original units.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: Children per adult and eyes per person both centre on about 2. What distinguishes them, and how would percentiles show it?
a:
The spread. For eyes the 10th and 50th percentiles are both 2 — no spread. For
children the 10th is 0 and the 50th is 2 — real spread.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: Why is the interquartile range described as more flexible than the standard deviation?
a:
Because the percentiles don't have to be 25 and 75 — you can compare the 10th and
90th, or any pair suited to the question.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: What is skew, and why does centre-plus-spread handle it badly?
a:
Skew is spread concentrated on one side. A centre and a spread implies symmetry, so
reporting only those hides the fact that one tail is long and the other isn't.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: What does taking a logarithm do to a skewed variable, and why bother?
a:
It shrinks the very large values inward, making the distribution more symmetric — so
mean-based calculations describe the data more accurately instead of being dominated by
the extreme values.
title: Summarizing Variables

q: What is a theoretical (sampling) distribution the distribution *of*?
a:
Of your result across repeated samples — how the statistic you computed would vary if
you redid the whole study over and over. Not of the data points within one sample.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: Why do we care about the theoretical distribution at all?
a:
Because its central tendency is the true parameter you're after — the true average, or
the true effect of X on Y.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: Which theoretical distribution describes coin flips? Averages? Counts?
a: Binomial, normal, and Poisson respectively.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: Why does the normal distribution show up so often?
a:
Because it describes the distribution of averages, and most of what we compute is some
kind of average.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: You flip two fair coins. Give the full theoretical distribution.
a: 50% one head and one tail (either order), 25% two heads, 25% two tails.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: You have one sample. What can it actually do for you?
a:
Narrow the range of plausible truths by ruling out theoretical distributions under
which your data would be very unlikely. It can't identify the true one.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: Define a p-value.
a:
The probability of observing your result or something more extreme, assuming the
theoretical distribution under consideration is correct.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: 100 flips, 40 heads, p = 2.8% one-tailed. What are the two possibilities?
a:
Either the assumed theoretical distribution is wrong (the coin isn't fair), or the
distribution is right and something unlikely happened.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: Why is the two-tailed version of that test 5.7% rather than 2.8%?
a:
Because it counts extremes in both directions — 40 heads or fewer *and* 60 heads or
more — rather than only the low side.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: After rejecting "fair coin", what do you now know about the true rate?
a:
Only that 50% is unlikely. You've eliminated a candidate, not identified the truth —
many other values remain live.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: What's the difference between the Bayesian and frequentist responses to surprising data?
a:
Bayesians update their belief about the world in the direction of the data.
Frequentists ask whether the data would be sufficiently unlikely under the candidate truth
and, if so, reject it.
title: Theoretical Distributions

q: What does it mean to condition on a variable?
a:
To hold it at a particular value and look at the distribution of another variable within
that restriction.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: State the definition of two variables being related.
a: Learning the value of one changes the distribution of the other.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: What's the definition of independence?
a:
Conditioning on one variable produces no change at all in the other's distribution — the
conditional and unconditional distributions are the same.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: Sex is ~50/50. You learn a stranger's name is Sarah. What has happened, in these terms?
a:
You conditioned on name, and the conditional distribution of sex shifted to roughly 95%
female. The shift is what makes name and sex related.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: You roll a die and get a 1. Why does this demonstrate independence?
a:
Because the distribution of the next roll is still 1/6 for every face — conditioning
changed nothing.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: About 15% take vitamin E. Among smokers the share is lower. What have you established, and what haven't you?
a:
Established: vitamin E use and smoking are statistically related. Not established:
anything about causation or direction.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: Why is vitamin E a suggestive example in the first place?
a:
It was recommended by health professionals only for a stretch, so uptake plausibly
tracked people who follow health advice generally — making it look related to healthy
outcomes because healthy people chose to take it.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: How do you condition on a continuous variable, and what's wrong with that?
a:
Bin it and compute a conditional mean within each bin — as with BMI bins versus share
taking vitamin E. The problem is the bins are arbitrary in both placement and number.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: Why is "proportion" already a conditional mean for a binary variable?
a:
Because the mean of a binary variable *is* its proportion, so computing a share within a
group is computing a conditional mean.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: Name three conditional summaries besides the conditional mean.
a:
Conditional median, conditional standard deviation, conditional skew, conditional
interquartile range — any summary statistic works within a slice.
title: Conditional Distributions

q: What does the height of a fitted line at a given X represent?
a: The conditional mean of Y at that value of X.
title: Line-Fitting

q: What was wrong with using bins to describe a relationship?
a:
The bin placement and count are arbitrary — different choices give different pictures, and
nothing in the data tells you which is right.
title: Line-Fitting

q: What is a LOESS curve doing mechanically?
a:
Rolling a very narrow window along the X axis and computing something like the average of Y
inside it at each point, producing a smooth curve without a pre-specified shape.
title: Line-Fitting

q: How does OLS differ from non-parametric regression in one sentence?
a:
OLS specifies the shape in advance and finds the best fit of that shape; non-parametric
methods let the data determine the shape.
title: Line-Fitting

q: Interpret `vitamin E = 0.110 + 0.002 × BMI`, both terms.
a:
At BMI 0 you'd predict 11% take vitamin E (mechanically — BMI 0 is impossible). Each
one-unit increase in BMI is associated with a 0.002 increase in the conditional mean of
vitamin E taking.
title: Line-Fitting

q: The LOESS curve rose steeply at low BMI and flattened at high BMI. What happens to that when you fit a straight line?
a:
It's lost. The straight line reports a single average slope across the range, discarding
the steep-then-shallow structure.
title: Line-Fitting

q: Give two reasons to accept that loss of detail.
a:
OLS coefficients have easy-to-describe sampling distributions, and simpler models are less
prone to fitting noise your data can't support.
title: Line-Fitting

q: What's the relationship between correlation and a regression slope?
a:
Correlation is a rescaled regression slope — the slope with units stripped out, running
from −1 to 1.
title: Line-Fitting

q: Does OLS force you into a straight line?
a: No — linear regression can fit curved shapes. That comes in Chapter 13.
title: Line-Fitting

q: What does a negative slope tell you?
a: The two variables are negatively related in a linear way.
title: Line-Fitting

q: What is a residual?
a:
The difference between an observation's actual value and the conditional mean you'd have
expected for it — the part the conditioning variable doesn't explain.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: Professors average 2.3 cups of coffee; Nick drinks 3. Identify the explained and unexplained parts.
a:
Explained: 2.3 cups, from knowing he's a professor. Residual: 0.7 cups, driven by something
other than being a professor.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: Why is the residual the useful part rather than a nuisance?
a:
Because it's variation guaranteed to have nothing to do with the variable you conditioned
on — so a relationship found inside it can't be explained by that variable.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: What is a conditional conditional distribution?
a:
The conditional distribution of Y given X, computed within the variation that remains after
conditioning on some other variable Z.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: Which everyday phrases mean the same as "conditioning on Z and looking at what remains"?
a: Controlling for Z, adjusting for Z, matching on Z.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: Shorts-wearers eat more ice cream; someone says it's just hot days. Describe the check.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: How do you compute a conditional conditional relationship manually?
a:
Use any relationship tool to explain X with Z and Y with Z, take both residuals, then examine
the relationship between the residuals.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: What's the shortcut in a regression?
a:
Regress Y on X *and* Z instead of X alone. The coefficient on X becomes the conditional
conditional relationship.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: BMI's coefficient goes from 0.002 to 0.001 when age and gender are added. Interpret both facts.
a:
The drop means part of the original BMI–vitamin E relationship was really age and gender.
The surviving 0.001 means that conditional on age and gender, a one-unit rise in BMI is still
associated with a 0.001 rise in the conditional mean of vitamin E taking.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: Your relationship survives controlling for Z. Have you shown causation?
a:
No. You eliminated one alternative explanation. Others remain — handling that systematically
is what identification is for.
title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables

q: Define identification.
a:
The link between the analysis you performed and the research question you wanted
answered — the process of ensuring your analysis answers *that* question and not a
different one.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: Why is "these variables are correlated, therefore one causes the other" not a statistics error?
a:
The correlation finding can be entirely correct, arrived at through proper statistical
steps. The failure is in the claim about what it answers — an identification error.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: What is a data generating process?
a:
The set of laws and relationships determining why you observed the data you did rather
than some other data. It operates whether or not you know about it.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: How does the planets-and-gravity story map onto empirical research?
a:
The observed paths are the data, the physical laws are the DGP, and working backwards
from paths to laws is what identification aims to do.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: Nick drops a pen. Give three explanations besides gravity that fit the data.
a:
Pens love the ground; he's secretly throwing it rather than dropping it; there's an
invisible wind pushing it down.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: What does the pen example demonstrate about observations?
a:
The same observations are consistent with many different data generating processes, so
the data alone can't tell you which one you're in.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: Nick pulls the lamp's string and nothing happens. What would you conclude, and why is it wrong?
a:
You'd conclude the string does nothing. It's wrong because the lamp isn't plugged in —
you needed to know lamps require electricity before you could interpret the observation.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: What's the lesson from the unplugged lamp?
a:
Data will lie to you if you don't understand the rest of the data generating process.
Accurate data can support a completely wrong conclusion.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: Why is it a problem that gravity isn't in your dataset?
a:
Because the thing driving the process is often unobservable — like ability or health
consciousness — so you have to reason about it rather than measure it.
title: Data Generating Processes

q: State the definition of causality being used here.
a:
If you reached in and changed the value of one variable, would the other change as a
result?
title: Alternative Explanations

q: What can you actually observe in data, as opposed to what you want?
a:
You observe a relationship — that the conditional mean of Y differs across values of X.
You want to know whether changing X changes Y, which the relationship alone doesn't tell you.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: Give three alternative explanations for the education–earnings relationship.
a:
People who'd be better workers anyway choose more education; people in richer areas both
get more education and earn more; people with natural aptitude for a high-paying field go to
school for it, so schooling rides along with aptitude.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: Does an alternative explanation mean your hypothesised effect is absent?
a:
No. The effect may be entirely real — it's just mixed in with other things, and you have
to isolate it.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: Why does randomization eliminate alternatives rather than merely adjust for them?
a:
Because assignment was made by a random device, so nothing about the people could have
influenced who got treated. The alternative never occurred in your data.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: Pulling a string is strongly related to a lamp turning on. Give an alternative explanation and the fix.
a:
Nick might be timing his pulls to when the light was going to change anyway. Fix: let a
coin decide whether to pull — the coin has no knowledge of the lamp — and see if the
relationship survives.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: Give the second lamp alternative and its fix.
a:
The string might control all the electricity in the house, so it changes the lamp via a
different mechanism. Fix: pull the string and check whether other things in the house switch
on and off at the same time.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: What besides randomization can close off an alternative explanation?
a:
Conditioning on a variable — the conditional conditional distributions from Ep 8 — plus
the many other designs covered later in the series.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: When have you identified a causal effect?
a:
When every alternative explanation has been ruled out or had its influence removed, and a
relationship still remains — so you know the analysis answers your research question.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: Why isn't a very strong relationship evidence of causality?
a:
Because alternative explanations can produce near-perfect relationships. The timed-pulls
story generates a strong relationship with no causal effect at all.
title: Alternative Explanations

q: What can a pile of contradictory studies on one topic indicate?
a:
That the analyses aren't actually answering the same question — they differ because
they're asking different questions, even when everyone intended the same one.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: What comparison generates the "one drink a day is good for you" finding?
a:
People having one drink a day are compared to people having zero, and the one-drink group
rates healthier.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: What's wrong with the zero-drinks comparison group?
a:
It contains people who are too sick to drink — whose poor health causes the non-drinking —
and ex-alcoholics still carrying the effects of past heavy drinking.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: What happens when Wood et al. remove those people and re-run?
a:
The apparent benefit of light drinking disappears. Little effect over the first few
drinks per week, then the hazard ratio climbs past roughly 100 grams of alcohol per week.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: What is a hazard ratio doing on the y-axis of their graph?
a: Higher means less healthy — more likely to die sooner.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: Beyond the composition of the zero group, what other alternatives did they handle?
a:
Background factors that drive both drinking and mortality — sex (men drink more and die
sooner for other reasons), plus class, race, location, and region. They adjust for things
like racial background.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: Define a placebo test.
a:
Running your method on a relationship that must be zero if your assumptions hold. A
non-zero result indicates something has been left out.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: What was the placebo test here, and what did it show?
a:
Same data and method, but with "probability of being a man" as the outcome. The method
implied that drinking more causes you to become a man — so an alternative explanation is
still unaccounted for.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: The alcohol–mortality effect is non-zero. What are the two possibilities, and can the placebo test choose between them?
a:
Either the causal effect is genuinely non-zero, or something else is still going on. The
placebo test can't choose — it only establishes that some of the second is present.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: What's the general lesson about when you're finished eliminating alternatives?
a:
There's always another one. Until you've narrowed down to exactly the part you want,
something is left over — and a placebo test is one way to detect that you're not there yet.
title: Alcohol and Mortality

q: State the working definition of causality used in causal inference.
a:
X causes Y if intervening to change the value of X would change the probability
distribution of Y.
title: Causality

q: Who developed causal diagrams, and what are they for?
a:
Judea Pearl. They're a way of writing a data generating process down so you can use it
to identify an effect.
title: Causality

q: Names and sex are strongly related. Why isn't that causal?
a:
Because intervening — persuading someone named Susan to become Denise — doesn't change
the distribution of their sex. Conditional relationship, no causal one.
title: Causality

q: Nick opens his hand and the pen falls. Was his hand *the* cause?
a:
No — a partial cause. Gravity and there being nothing underneath were causes too. An
arrow only ever claims "a cause".
title: Causality

q: There's a wet spot outside a store and nobody slips. Did the wet spot cause slipping?
a:
In causal inference terms, yes — it raised the probability of someone falling, which is
what causing means. A lawyer would say no, because no event occurred.
title: Causality

q: You flip a light switch and nothing happens because the bulb is burned out. Does the switch cause the light?
a:
Yes. Causes change distributions rather than guaranteeing outcomes, so a failure on one
occasion doesn't revoke the causal relationship.
title: Causality

q: Someone gets a scholarship, goes to college, then does exactly what they'd have done anyway. Did college cause their occupation?
a:
Yes — it changed the distribution of occupations available to them, raising the
probability of outcomes like becoming a lawyer, even though they landed in the same place.
title: Causality

q: List some words that assert a relationship without asserting causality.
a:
Associated, correlated, related, tend to occur together, tend not to occur together, go
together.
title: Causality

q: List some words that assert causality.
a: Causes, affects, the effect of, increases, decreases, changes, leads to, determines.
title: Causality

q: What structural feature distinguishes causal language from non-causal language?
a:
Direction. "X causes Y" and "Y causes X" are different statements; "X is related to Y"
and "Y is related to X" are the same one.
title: Causality

q: Why are "linked to" and "followed by" worth being suspicious of?
a:
They imply a direction without technically claiming causality — planting a causal idea
where the evidence doesn't support one.
title: Causality

q: What is a causal diagram, precisely?
a:
A set of variables relevant to your question, plus arrows between them, each arrow
meaning one variable causes the other.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: Is `red hair` a variable? Explain.
a: No. `Hair color` is the variable; red is one of the values it can take.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: Where do the arrows in a causal diagram come from?
a: From theory — your understanding of the setting. Not from the data.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: State the rule for when to draw an arrow.
a: Anything that changes the distribution of a variable should be drawn as a cause of it.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: Can one variable cause two things? Can one variable have two causes?
a:
Both, and both are ordinary. The coin flip causes cake and money; money is caused by
both the coin flip and Terry being in the room.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: Should unmeasurable variables appear on the diagram?
a:
Yes. Terry's mood can't be measured but genuinely causes whether Terry is in the room,
so it goes on — usually greyed out to signal it's unobserved.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: What is `U1` doing on a diagram?
a:
Standing in for an unknown unmeasured factor causing two variables — for example
something causing both shorts-wearing and ice-cream-eating, which makes them related with
no direct arrow between them.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: Give the two pathways from police to crime that you *want* to count.
a:
Police → Crime (directly stopping crimes in progress) and Police → Expected crime payout
→ Crime (deterrence — criminals think they're less likely to get away with it).
title: Causal Diagrams

q: Give the pathway from police to crime that you *don't* want, and why.
a:
Police ← Lagged crime → Crime. High-crime areas get assigned more police, and past crime
predicts current crime, so the two move together for reasons unrelated to policing.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: What's the real value of drawing a causal diagram?
a:
It makes the pathways between two variables visible, so you can sort them into the ones
that answer your question and the ones that don't, then work out how to eliminate the
latter.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: Two variables have no arrow between them. Can they still be related?
a:
Yes — a shared cause such as `U1` will make them move together without any direct causal
link.
title: Causal Diagrams

q: What are the two steps in drawing a causal diagram?
a:
List the variables relevant to the data generating process, then use prior knowledge and
research to decide which variables cause which, and draw the arrows.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Why can't you draw a causal diagram without making assumptions?
a:
The arrows come from prior knowledge and previous research. If you refuse to rest on
anything you already believe, there's nothing to draw arrows from — and you can't learn more
without staking something.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Which variables are the highest priority to include?
a:
Ones that cause both the treatment and the outcome, and — more broadly — anything at all
that causes the treatment.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Why does anything causing the treatment matter so much?
a:
Because much of causal inference depends on understanding why different people got
different levels of treatment. The less you understand that, the more back-door problems you
let in.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: In the online-courses study, why do work hours have to be on the diagram?
a:
They cause both. Working a lot makes an online course more attractive (it doesn't clash
with the job) and makes dropping out more likely (too much time, or a promotion removes the
need for the degree).
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: You realise living near a quiet café slightly affects whether people take online courses. Include it?
a:
No. It's a genuine cause for a handful of people but not a major determinant, and in
social science the list of faintly relevant causes is infinite.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Should unmeasurable variables like "preferences about education" be included?
a: Yes. Whether you can measure something has nothing to do with whether it causes things.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Do arrows only run into the treatment and outcome?
a:
No — variables cause each other. Socioeconomic status causes preferences, and it causes
internet access.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Why draw `U5` causing both socioeconomic status and location instead of an arrow between them?
a:
Because you wouldn't confidently claim either direction. They're related, so you give them
a common cause rather than assert a causal direction you can't defend.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Your first diagram is a chaotic mess. What does that mean?
a:
Nothing has gone wrong — that's the normal first result, because in social science
everything relates to everything. Simplification is the next step.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Why is it better to base arrows on previous research than on your own reasoning?
a:
It makes the diagram defensible and helps a field converge on the same diagram, rather
than everyone drawing from private intuition.
title: Drawing Causal Diagrams

q: Name the four ways to simplify a causal diagram.
a: Unimportance, redundancy, removing mediators, and irrelevance.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: What justifies dropping unimportant variables?
a:
Statistics is about modelling the world, not perfectly representing it. Every assumption is
at least slightly false, so losing a trivial distraction to see the important structure is a
good trade.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: When can several variables be bucketed into one?
a:
When their arrows go into and out of the same places, so from the point of view of your
research question they play the same role — like race, gender, and SES becoming `demographics`.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: Does bucketing variables pretend the world is simple?
a:
No. It packages the complexity into one labelled node so you can see what the problem is.
You'll unpack it when you actually use the diagram.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: What is a mediator?
a:
A variable sitting between two others that explains *why* one causes the other — like
cigarettes smoked between cigarette price and lung cancer.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: When do you keep a mediator and when do you cut it?
a:
Keep it if the mechanism is your research question — if smoking is your treatment, it stays.
Cut it if you're studying price → cancer and aren't investigating the mechanism.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: Give the online-courses mediators Nick removes.
a:
How affiliated you feel with the college (never being on campus, so you don't think of
yourself as one of the Fighting Lions) and differences in education quality.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: What is the irrelevance criterion?
a:
A variable not on any pathway between your treatment and outcome can be dropped, even though
it is genuinely part of the data generating process.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: Give the irrelevance example.
a:
A college programme structured so students end up needing one extra term to graduate. It
really does cause dropout, but has nothing to do with taking online courses, so it comes off.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: Why is deciding what to ignore described as the actual skill?
a:
Because you can't account for the full complexity of the world. Refuse to ignore anything and
the only conclusion available is "the world sure is complex" — anything you can justifiably
ignore is leverage.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: How did the simplified online diagram reveal it had gone too far?
a:
It left no pathway between demographics and location except through a collider, implying
their correlation should be zero. Different locations plainly have different racial mixes and
age distributions, so that's false.
title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams

q: Define a path on a causal diagram.
a:
Any walk from one variable to another along the diagram. You may travel backwards up an
arrow, and you may not visit a variable twice.
title: Causal Pathways

q: The arrow runs B → treatment. Is "treatment to B" a legal step in a path?
a: Yes. Paths can be walked against the direction of an arrow.
title: Causal Pathways

q: Why is `treatment → B → A → treatment → B → C → outcome` not a path?
a: It doubles back, visiting treatment twice.
title: Causal Pathways

q: What does each path represent?
a: A separate reason the two endpoint variables would be related to each other in your data.
title: Causal Pathways

q: Define front door and back door.
a:
A front door is a path where all arrows point away from the treatment toward the outcome.
A back door has at least one arrow somewhere along it pointing back at the treatment.
title: Causal Pathways

q: In the wine diagram, give the two front door paths.
a: Wine → Lifespan, and Wine → Drugs → Lifespan.
title: Causal Pathways

q: Tell the story for the path Wine ← Health → Lifespan.
a:
Healthier people feel able to drink more (if you're really sick you avoid alcohol), and
being healthier also means living longer — so wine and lifespan correlate without wine doing
anything.
title: Causal Pathways

q: What's the difference between a front door path and a good path?
a:
Front door is structural — it's about arrow directions. Good is about your research
question — whether you want that path counted.
title: Causal Pathways

q: Give an example of a front door path that is a bad path.
a:
If your question is the effect of wine on lifespan *excluding* its effect on drug taking,
then Wine → Drugs → Lifespan is a front door you want to shut.
title: Causal Pathways

q: What does a back door path indicate about your treatment?
a:
That something other than random variation is driving it — something upstream is
influencing who gets treated, and that something reaches the outcome by its own route.
title: Causal Pathways

q: State what identification means in path terms.
a:
Close all the bad paths and leave all the good paths open, so the only variation remaining
in the data is the variation you wanted to measure.
title: Causal Pathways

q: When is a path open, and when is it closed?
a:
Open when every variable on it is allowed to vary in your sample. Closed when at least one
variable on it has no variation.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: What are the two ways to close a path?
a:
Condition or control for a variable on it, or select a sample in which that variable has no
variation.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: You control for health. Which paths close?
a: Every path that health sits on, all at once — not just the one you had in mind.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: In the wine diagram, controlling for income closes which paths, and what's left?
a:
Paths 3, 4, and 6 — every back door income sits on. Left open: the two front doors plus
path 5, Wine ← Health → Lifespan.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: Why must you control for health specifically to close path 5?
a:
Health is the only variable on that path, so there's nothing else you could control for to
shut it.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: Why should you *not* control for drugs?
a:
Drugs is on the front door path Wine → Drugs → Lifespan, which you want counted. Closing it
removes part of the real effect.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: What is post-treatment bias?
a:
Controlling for a variable that is affected by your treatment, which closes a front door
path and deletes part of the effect you're trying to measure.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: Define a collider.
a: A variable on a path where both arrows on that path point toward it.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: What's the default status of a path containing a collider, and what changes it?
a: It's pre-closed — no action needed. Controlling for the collider opens it.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: Is "collider" a property of a variable or of a variable on a path?
a:
Of a variable on a particular path. The same variable can be a collider on one path and not
on another.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: Tutoring → College ← Intelligence. You survey only college students. What happens and why?
a:
You've controlled for college, opening the path. Within college students, tutoring and being
smart are substitute routes in, so they appear negatively related — even if unrelated in the
population.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: You randomized your treatment. Are you safe from collider bias?
a:
No. Tutoring was randomized and the college-student sample still produces a spurious negative
relationship, entirely from sample selection.
title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables

q: What assumption underlies the whole close-the-bad-paths procedure?
a: That the diagram is a reasonable representation of the actual data generating process.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: Define a placebo test.
a:
Taking your assumptions, deriving from them a relationship or effect that should be zero,
and checking. A non-zero result means an assumption is wrong.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: Give the advertising placebo test.
a:
Run the same design on *last* month's sales. This month's advertising cannot have raised
them, so the effect should be zero; if it isn't, the design is missing something.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: How does any causal diagram generate placebo tests?
a:
Pick any two variables, list every path between them, close them all. Since paths are any
reason two variables are related, the observed relationship should then be zero.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: What does a non-zero result on such a test tell you?
a:
There's a path you left off the diagram, or a variable you should be conditioning on —
meaning you don't yet know what to control for to identify your real effect.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: In the wine diagram, why can you ignore every income–drugs path running through lifespan?
a:
Income points into lifespan and drugs points into lifespan, so lifespan is a collider on
those paths and they're pre-closed.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: What single variable closes the remaining income–drugs paths?
a: Wine — look only at wine drinkers, only at non-drinkers, or condition on wine statistically.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: How can you tell this particular test will fail before running it?
a:
Because the diagram claims the only reason income relates to drug taking is via wine's
effect on drugs, which is plainly insufficient.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: What is the actual goal for your assumptions, if not being correct?
a:
Being close enough to correct that they don't mess you up too much — perfect assumptions are
impossible.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: Name three legitimate responses to failing a placebo test.
a:
Add the missing variables to the diagram and redo the path analysis; judge the violation
small enough not to matter; or accept the problem and disclose it to readers of your study.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests

q: Why are colliders especially relevant when testing pairs that aren't the treatment and outcome?
a:
Because many paths between two arbitrary variables run into a common effect, making that
variable a collider and the path pre-closed — so there's much less to do than the path count
suggests.
title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests
